text: The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and identifies a healthy colonic loop and a large parastomal hernia. Minimal adhesions from previous surgery are visible. Pelvic adhesions between small bowel loops and the rectal stump are divided using gentle blunt dissection. Minor bands in the left lower quadrant are also divided, and the colostomy is freed from the anterolateral abdominal wall. The colostomy is disconnected from the skin edges and fascia, revealing a small abscess cavity with a stoma fistula at the superior mucocutaneous border. The cavity is debrided to healthy adipose tissue, and the hernia defect is repaired with interrupted non-absorbable sutures.

The surgeon re-establishes pneumoperitoneum and mobilizes the left colon up to the splenic flexure, ensuring sufficient length for a tension-free anastomosis. The base of the colonic mesentery is also mobilized, with careful dissection to avoid injury to the ureter or colonic vessels.

The surgeon carefully isolates the rectal stump from the posterior aspect of the bladder without using sharp or energy-based dissection. A circular stapler is inserted through the anus, and an anastomosis is performed using the Knight-Griffin technique of double stapling. The colon reaches the pelvis without tension, and care is taken to ensure it is not twisted before closing the stapler. The stapler is fired and gently removed through the anus. A drain is placed in the pelvis, and the parietal peritoneum is closed with intracorporeal sutures.
